title: Colonial Sydney
blurb: The first sixty years, in order — the founding, the convict system, the Eora, the rebels and the emancipists who built a city out of a gaol.

stops.note: 26 January 1788: the First Fleet drops anchor at Warrane, Gadigal country. A penal camp of a thousand convicts and marines becomes the seed of a city.
stops.quote: “We had the satisfaction of finding the finest harbour in the world, in which a thousand sail of the line may ride in the most perfect security.” — Governor Arthur Phillip, 1788

stops.note: On the rise above the cove, Governor Phillip raises the colony's first Government House — the seat of power, looking down on the convict camp below.
The whole settlement depends on one stream of fresh water. Convicts cut storage tanks into its sandstone bed; for a generation it is Sydney's lifeline.
Within weeks, hunger and order collide: a teenage convict, Thomas Barrett, is hanged for stealing food — the colony's first execution.
Phillip needs to understand the Eora whose land this is. The Wangal man Bennelong becomes go-between for two worlds — and gives his name to the point across the cove.
The camp is near starvation until, at Parramatta in 1789, the ex-convict James Ruse proves the soil can yield a crop — and earns the colony's first land grant.
On the western point, Lieutenant Dawes sets up an observatory and the colony's first gun battery — science and defence at the harbour mouth.
1804: driven past endurance, Irish convicts rise at Castle Hill. The redcoats crush them at 'Vinegar Hill' — the first rebellion on Australian soil.
1808: the officers of the Rum Corps, who run the colony's trade in spirits, march on Government House and arrest Governor Bligh — the only armed coup in the nation's history.
Order restored, a new class rises: the emancipists. Mary Reibey, transported at thirteen for horse theft, builds a trading empire and becomes the colony's merchant queen.
Down at the waterline stands Cadman's Cottage (1816), home to the convict crews of the government boats — today the oldest house in the city.
Governor Macquarie now sets the convicts building in stone. His grandest scheme is a hospital funded by a monopoly on rum (1811–16) — its wings survive as the Mint and Parliament House.
At South Head in 1818, Macquarie's architect — the convict forger Francis Greenway — raises Australia's first lighthouse.
And from 1819 the convicts themselves sleep in Greenway's barracks on Macquarie Street, marched out to government labour each day.
The convict women have their own institution: the Parramatta Female Factory (1821) — workhouse, marriage market and refuge in one.
The hand behind so much of this city is Francis Greenway, transported for forgery and freed by Macquarie on the strength of his buildings — Australia's first great architect.
Wealth, not just stone, transforms the emancipists: Samuel Terry, another ex-convict, grows so rich on land and rum that he is called 'the Botany Bay Rothschild'.
And at Vaucluse, the native-born W. C. Wentworth turns emancipist confidence into politics — his committee will draft the constitution that wins the colony self-government in 1853.

title: Tour The Rocks
blurb: One square kilometre, every layer of the city's story — plague, pubs, demolition and defiance.

stops.note: Start at the cove. Everything in The Rocks flows uphill from this shoreline.
The water once lapped at this door — everything between here and the Quay is reclaimed land.
Up into the terraces: four houses, 150 years of working families, preserved as found.
Around the corner, an entire colonial neighbourhood excavated and left open under a hostel on stilts.
These streets emptied in 1900 — plague, resumption, demolition. The photographs taken then are why we know them.
Climb to the top: windmill, fort, observatory — and the best free view in Sydney.
Pause at the Hero of Waterloo — and ask the bar staff about the trapdoor in the cellar.
Down to the point beneath the bridge, where the colony first mounted guns.
End at the corner named for the man whose green bans saved everything you just walked through.
And above it all, the arch that demolished half the neighbourhood to rise — and made the rest immortal.

title: Dark Sydney
blurb: Shipwreck, gibbet, razor gangs and unsolved cases — the city's shadow history.

stops.note: Begin at The Gap on a stormy thought: 121 souls lost against these cliffs in one night of 1857.
Across to North Head, where the quarantined carved their names — and many never left. Sydney's most haunted address, by reputation.
On this picnic beach, an assassin shot a prince in the back in 1868.
Out on the water: Pinchgut, where a hanged man swung in chains for years as a warning.
The grinning Face hides the park's darkest night — the Ghost Train fire of 1979.
Into Kings Cross, where the Witch faced the obscenity squads of the 1950s.
Victoria Street, 1975: the publisher who fought the developers and vanished. No body, no conviction.
Palmer Street — the Queen of the loophole, her empire run from these terraces.
Over the hill in Surry Hills, her great rival: sly grog, cocaine, and Christmas parties for the children.
To Coogee, where a captured shark coughed up a tattooed arm — and a murder case.
End in Bondi with the kidnapping that ended an innocence — and changed the lottery laws.

title: Rebels & Bohemians
blurb: Picture palaces, pub philosophers, soapbox orators and the night march that became Mardi Gras.

stops.note: Start under the chandelier of the Empire's greatest theatre — the survivor of Sydney's picture-palace age.
A block south stood the Troc, where the city jitterbugged through the war. The wreckers took it in 1971.
Down Sussex Street to the Royal George, where the Push argued Greer, James and Hughes into existence.
Through Martin Place, Bea Miles territory — Shakespeare, a shilling a sonnet.
Into the Domain, parliament of the people: every Sunday, every creed, heckled equally.
Up to the Cross, where bohemia lived dangerously — and Goossens' opera-house dream burned beside Norton's scandal.
Darlinghurst, before dawn: one word in perfect copperplate, half a million times.
Finish at Taylor Square: 24 June 1978, 53 arrests — and the parade that answers them every year since.

title: Sporting Sydney
blurb: From the swim that broke the law to the night Cathy Freeman ran — beaches, tracks and grandstands.

stops.note: Begin at Manly, where a newspaper editor's illegal midday swim invented Australian beach culture.
Across to Bondi: once the ocean was legal, someone had to get everyone out alive. The reel and flags start here.
Mid-beach, 1938: three freak waves, 250 rescues in minutes — lifesaving's greatest hour.
At the south end, the winter swimmers' pool — three Sundays a month, five years, no excuses.
Down the coast at Wylie's Baths, where the fastest woman in the water trained for Stockholm 1912.
Behind the dunes, the ghost of the killer track — a concrete saucer the suburb swallowed.
Inland to Randwick, racing since 1833 on the old Sandy Course.
To the SCG, stage of cricket's doomed artist.
Same turf, 1932: the summer England aimed at the batsman and the Empire wobbled.
At Rushcutters Bay stood the tin shed where Jack Johnson changed boxing — and history — on Boxing Day 1908.
Across the city to Balmain's tidal baths, home of three straight Olympic golds.
Finish at Homebush: from abattoir to the night 112,000 people watched Cathy Freeman run the 400.

title: Birth of a City
blurb: How a penal camp became a town — from Cook's landing to Macquarie's Sydney, in the order it happened.

stops.note: April 1770: the Endeavour anchors at Kamay (Botany Bay). Cook claims the east coast — and eighteen years later the British return to stay.
January 1788: as the First Fleet arrives, two French ships sail in behind them. For six weeks the rivals share Botany Bay — but the British have already moved on.
Phillip finds Botany Bay too poor and rows north into Port Jackson. On 21 January 1788 his party spends the first night ashore here, inside the Heads.
Five days later the whole fleet moves up to Warrane — deep water and a freshwater stream. On 26 January 1788 the flag goes up and Sydney begins.
Everything depends on that stream. Convicts cut storage tanks into its bed, and the infant town clusters along its banks.
On the rise above the cove, Phillip raises the colony's first Government House — the seat of power for the next half-century.
The camp is starving. At Parramatta in 1789 the ex-convict James Ruse proves the land can feed the colony, and earns the first land grant.
Upriver, the government farm at Toongabbie works convict gangs hard to grow the grain that keeps Sydney alive.
1804: pushed too far, Irish convicts rise at Castle Hill. The redcoats crush them at 'Vinegar Hill' — the colony's first rebellion.
Governor Macquarie, arriving in 1810, sets the convicts building in stone. His grandest scheme is a hospital paid for in rum (1811–16), whose wings survive as the Mint and Parliament House.
At South Head in 1818, his architect Greenway raises Australia's first lighthouse — the convict forger's freedom, and a rough camp's coming of age.
And from 1819 the convicts sleep in Greenway's barracks on Macquarie Street, marched to government work each day — the convict city made solid.

title: Rum, Rebellion & a Stolen Governor
blurb: The grog that ran the colony — and the morning the army arrested the King's own governor.

stops.note: By 1806 the real currency of New South Wales is rum, and the officers of the NSW Corps control the trade. Into this walks Governor William Bligh — of Bounty fame — determined to break their grip.
26 January 1808, twenty years to the day after the founding: the Corps marches on Government House and arrests Bligh. Australia's only armed seizure of government — the Rum Rebellion.
London sends Macquarie to restore order in 1810 — and he promptly strikes his own rum bargain, funding a grand hospital by selling three merchants a three-year monopoly on the spirit trade.
The hospital's south wing still stands: later the colonial branch of the Royal Mint, where gold from the diggings became sovereigns.
Its north wing became the Parliament of New South Wales — the rum hospital now home to the state's lawmakers.
Macquarie's building spree rolled on — barracks, churches and courthouses, almost all from the hand of his emancipist architect, Francis Greenway.
And the emancipist cause Bligh's enemies feared came good: at Vaucluse, W. C. Wentworth's committee drafted the constitution that won the colony self-government in 1853.

title: How Sydney Got Its Water
blurb: Follow the city's thirst across a century — from a fouled stream to a tunnel, a swamp and a great reservoir.

stops.note: 1788: Sydney is founded on this one freshwater stream. Within a generation the growing town fouls it with refuse and run-off.
By the 1820s the Tank Stream is an open drain. The colony turns to the Lachlan Swamps — today's Centennial Park — for clean water.
From 1827 convicts hand-dig a tunnel nearly four kilometres from the swamps to Hyde Park: Busby's Bore, the city's first piped supply, finished 1837.
The city outgrows the bore too. The Upper Nepean Scheme dams distant rivers and banks the water at Prospect Reservoir, opened 1888.
And from 1891, great steam engines at Ryde pump that water uphill to slake the booming northern suburbs.

title: Defending the Harbour
blurb: A century and a half of guns on the water — from the first colonial battery to the night the war came to Sydney.

stops.note: 1791: the colony's first gun battery, on the point the Gadigal called Tarra, guarding the mouth of the cove.
For decades just a rock and a gibbet; a scare over Russian and American warships finally fortifies it in 1857 as a Martello tower — Fort Denison.
stops.quote: “The last Martello tower built anywhere in the British Empire.” — on Fort Denison's 1857 gun tower

stops.note: As the colony grows, sandstone batteries ring the harbour. From the 1870s the guns of Bradleys Head command the approach up-harbour.
Beside it, the great works of Middle Head — rings of forts and tunnels dug into the headland.
And the Georges Head battery alongside, its guns trained back toward the Heads.
Further in, Steele Point's battery covers the inner reaches from the Nielsen Park shore.
Out at Botany Bay, Bare Island's concrete fort (1885) guards the colony's undefended 'back door'.
The explosives that feed the guns are kept safely up Middle Harbour, in the magazines of Bantry Bay from 1915.
Then war comes home. In 1942 an anti-submarine boom net is strung clear across the harbour, Georges Head to Watsons Bay.
On the night of 31 May 1942, three Japanese midget submarines slip past it. The war has reached Sydney Harbour.
And on South Head, the navy's wartime base — now HMAS Watson — trains the warfare officers who keep the watch still.

title: Sydney Takes to the Surf
blurb: How the beach was won — from an illegal daylight swim to the world's first surf lifesavers and a day of disaster.

stops.note: From the 1870s, sea-bathing means sheltered ocean pools cut into the rocks — McIver's at Coogee, a women's and children's bath in use since 1876.
In the harbour at Balmain, the tidal baths open in the 1880s — where, generations later, a young Dawn Fraser will learn to swim her way to Olympic gold.
But the open surf is banned by day as indecent. In 1902–03 at Manly, W. H. Gocher defies the ban and swims at noon — and wins the beach for everyone.
With the coast suddenly fashionable, in 1906 a vast amusement park — Wonderland City — opens on the clifftops above Tamarama.
The open surf is deadly, though. At Bondi in 1907 locals form a club and invent surf lifesaving — the reel, the line and the belt.
That same year the champion swimmer Henry Wylie cuts his own ocean baths into the Coogee rocks.
Between the wars the beach booms. Coogee throws an English pleasure pier out over the surf in 1928.
And the hardiest swimmers form the Bondi Icebergs in 1929, vowing to swim right through the winter.
Then, on 6 February 1938, freak waves drag hundreds out at Bondi. The lifesavers' greatest rescue — yet five are lost. 'Black Sunday'.

title: Protest & the People's Sydney
blurb: Two centuries of Sydney pushing back — from an armed coup to the Redfern Address.

stops.note: 1808: the colony's first uprising, when the Rum Corps overthrew Governor Bligh — Sydney's habit of pushing back, born early.
1855: stonemasons down tools and win the eight-hour day, one of the world's first — and march to celebrate it every year after.
1890: the Great Maritime Strike pits unions against employers on the wharves. Its defeat drives workers into politics and the new Labor Party.
1917: a new time-card at Eveleigh sparks the Great Strike — some 100,000 walk out across the state, the largest stoppage the country had seen.
1931: the Depression throws families onto the street. At Newtown and Bankstown the unemployed barricade homes and fight police to stop the evictions.
26 January 1938: as the city marks 150 years, Aboriginal leaders gather in protest — the Day of Mourning, a founding moment of the land-rights movement.
1971: Hunters Hill women trying to save a last patch of bush win the world's first 'green ban', as builders' labourers refuse to destroy it.
That same year, huge crowds besiege the all-white Springbok rugby tour, helping turn Australia against apartheid.
1978: a night march down Oxford Street ends in arrests and beatings — and becomes, in defiance, the first Mardi Gras.
1992: at Redfern Park, Paul Keating tells white Australia the truth of dispossession — 'We took the traditional lands.'

title: The Healing City
blurb: How Sydney learned to care for its sick — from a hospital paid for in rum to the women who built their own.

stops.note: 1816: Macquarie builds the colony a hospital, funded by a monopoly on rum. Its central block is the seed of today's Sydney Hospital.
1832: to keep disease off the arriving ships, the Quarantine Station opens at North Head — a place of fear, and of relief, for new migrants.
1878: a kinder idea of care — the asylum at Callan Park, built on the humane 'Kirkbride' plan as a hospital in a garden.
1888: on a tiny harbour island, Louis Pasteur's team tests a microbe to end the rabbit plague — world-class science in Sydney's backyard.
1893: the Crown Street Women's Hospital opens, where generations of Sydneysiders will be born.
1900: bubonic plague breaks out by the wharves. The city cleanses and burns The Rocks in panic — and modern public health is born.
1906: the children's hospital settles at Camperdown, caring for the city's youngest patients.
1922: women doctors, long shut out, open the Rachel Forster Hospital — staffed and run by women, for women.

title: Federation to Olympics
blurb: The making of the modern city — from the birth of a nation to the best Games ever.

stops.note: 1 January 1901: in Centennial Park the colonies become a nation. The Commonwealth of Australia is sworn in, in a Sydney park.
1932: after years of labour through the Depression, the Harbour Bridge joins the two shores — the 'Coathanger', the city's defining shape.
At its opening a sword-wielding zealot gallops up and slashes the ribbon before the Premier can — the strangest moment in Sydney's story.
1935: across the water beneath the bridge, Luna Park opens its grinning face — mass fun, just for fun.
1959–73: at Bennelong Point, Utzon's white sails rise through a saga of genius, politics and resignation — and become the emblem of the nation.
1988: for the Bicentenary, the worn-out working docks of Darling Harbour are remade as a waterfront playground.
1992: a second crossing dives beneath the harbour to ease the bridge's load.
2000: on a remediated industrial wasteland at Homebush, Sydney stages the Olympic Games — declared the 'best ever'.
And weeks later the Paralympics, whose sell-out Sydney crowds changed the movement for good.

title: The Working Harbour
blurb: The harbour that built the city — whales, docks, sugar and coal, before the cargo ships moved on.

stops.note: 1830s: long before the suburbs, Mosman Bay is a whaling station, boiling down the catch on the harbour's edge.
1839: the prison island of Cockatoo becomes a great dockyard, building and mending ships for more than a century.
1855: at Balmain, Thomas Mort builds Australia's first dry dock — the colony learns to build its own ships.
1878: the Colonial Sugar Refinery rises at Pyrmont — 'sugar town', refining the sweetness of the Pacific.
The great finger wharves of Walsh Bay handle the wool and cargo that make Sydney rich.
1920: at Balls Head a giant coal loader fuels the steamers through tunnels cut into the headland.
Along the waterfront, wharfies queue for a day's hire on the 'Hungry Mile' — and in the Depression, for any work at all.
By the 1980s the container ship has killed the old working harbour, and Darling Harbour's wharves give way to crowds and apartments.

title: Migrant Sydney
blurb: The waves that remade the city — from gold-rush gardeners to the post-war 'New Australians'.

stops.note: From the 1850s gold rushes, Chinese diggers shut out of the fields turn to market gardening, feeding the city from plots on its sandy fringe — Sydney's first great non-British community.
Pushed about by prejudice, their community shifts west across the city over the decades, settling at last around Dixon Street: the Chinatown that endures today.
In the 1890s, Syrian and Lebanese families — the first Arabic-speaking migrants — build a quarter around Redfern, hawking and trading their way up.
After the Second World War the floodgates open. The Maltese gather first at Woolloomooloo, by the wharves where their ships came in (from 1948).
The 'New Australians' of the great post-war scheme are housed in Nissen-hut hostels like Bradfield Park, learning a new country in tin sheds.
At East Hills and a dozen others, displaced Europeans wait out their first Australian years on the city's edge.
As they move out into the suburbs, communities take root: Italians make Leichhardt 'Little Italy', with its espresso bars and delicatessens.
Greeks pour into the inner west through the 1950s, turning Marrickville into 'the Athens of the south'.
By the 1960s a small Spanish quarter grows on Liverpool Street, around its club and tapas bars.
And the Portuguese make Petersham 'Little Portugal' — its cake shops and charcoal chicken still draw the crowds.

title: First Nations: Deep Time to Today
blurb: The oldest story on the map — sixty thousand years of Country, dispossession and survival, from the rock engravings to the Redfern Address.

stops.note: For thousands of years Eora women fished the harbour from bark nawi canoes, small fires burning aboard — everyday life on a Country already ancient when the British came.
On the north shore lived the Cammeraygal, a powerful clan of the coastal Eora; the harbour's peoples were bound together by trade, kinship and law.
Across the sandstone they carved their world — at North Bondi, whales and ancestors cut into the headland, part of a sacred landscape made over millennia.
26 January 1788: the First Fleet anchors at Warrane. For the Gadigal, it is the day the world begins to change.
1789: Phillip has a Gadigal man, Arabanoo, seized to learn the language. He dies months later in the smallpox that soon devastates the Eora.
Bennelong, taken next, becomes the colony's interpreter between two worlds — and gives his name to the point where the Opera House now stands.
His wife Barangaroo refuses the colony entirely — its clothes, its food, its cruelties — a defiance now honoured on the headland named for her.
From the 1790s the Bidjigal warrior Pemulwuy leads a twelve-year war of resistance against the settlers, until he is shot dead in 1802.
1816: as the frontier pushes south-west, soldiers drive Dharawal and Gandangara people over the gorge at Appin — a massacre carried out on Macquarie's orders.
Survivors' children are taken to the 'Native Institution' to be remade; among them Maria Lock fights, in the colony's own courts, to hold her people's land.
Into the 20th century, families pushed off the reserves gather at independent camps like Salt Pan Creek — a seedbed of modern Aboriginal activism.
26 January 1938: a century and a half on, Aboriginal leaders declare a Day of Mourning and demand citizenship and rights.
1973: at Redfern, the Aboriginal Housing Company wins 'the Block' — Aboriginal-owned land in the heart of the city.
1992: at Redfern Park a Prime Minister finally speaks the truth of dispossession — and the oldest story on this map turns toward reckoning.

title: Vanished Sydney
blurb: The grand and the gaudy that Sydney tore down or lost — a tour of landmarks that aren't there any more.

stops.note: 1788: the colony's first Government House stood here for half a century, then was demolished — only its footings remain, traced in the pavement.
1879: the soaring, domed Garden Palace dazzled the Exhibition crowds — until it burned to the ground in a single hour in September 1882.
For seventy years the Australia Hotel was the place to be seen in Sydney — swept away in 1971 for the MLC Centre.
By 1900 Anthony Hordern's 'Palace Emporium' on Brickfield Hill was among the largest shops on earth, under the motto 'While I Live I'll Grow'. A tower stands there now.
Out at Tamarama, the vast Wonderland City ran from 1906 — its airships, slides and even an elephant now utterly vanished.
The Glaciarium gave Sydney its first indoor ice from 1907 — skating under one roof, until it melted from the map.
The 'Old Tin Shed' at Rushcutters Bay roared with boxing and rock'n'roll for sixty years, until the Eastern Suburbs railway swept it away.
Across the city, Mark Foy's Piazza store drew the crowds in Edwardian splendour; the building survives as a courthouse, but the great emporium is long gone.
Coogee's English pleasure pier reached out over the surf from 1928 — battered by storms, it was gone within six years.
And the Regent, Sydney's grandest picture palace, fell to the wreckers in 1988 — despite a green ban fought to save it.
And at Manly, the Fun Pier met the ferries with dodgems and a giant shark's-mouth doorway, until the wharf was rebuilt in the 1980s.
The Trocadero dance palace swept the war years along George Street with its sprung floor — demolished in 1971.

title: Crime & Scandal
blurb: Two centuries of the city's darkest headlines — from the first hanging to the bikie massacre.

stops.note: February 1788, weeks after landing: a teenager, Thomas Barrett, is hanged for stealing food — the colony's first execution.
1826: two soldiers commit a crime to escape the army; Governor Darling's brutal punishment kills one of them — and ignites the colony's fight for a free press.
1895: a ferry-master is convicted of poisoning his wife, pardoned amid huge public sympathy — then confesses. The 'Lemon Syrup Case' grips the city.
1920: the sensational trial of Eugenia Falleni, who had lived for decades as a man — a case that reveals more about its era than its accused.
Through the 1920s and '30s the razor gangs run Sydney's vice. Tilly Devine rules the brothels of Darlinghurst...
...while her great rival Kate Leigh runs the sly-grog and cocaine. Their war keeps the city's razors busy.
1935: a shark at the Coogee aquarium disgorges a human arm — and a tattoo launches Sydney's strangest murder mystery.
1960: weeks after his father wins the Opera House lottery, eight-year-old Graeme Thorne is kidnapped and killed — the case that ended an Australian innocence.
1963: two bodies are found by the Lane Cove River in circumstances no one can explain — the Bogle–Chandler mystery, unsolved to this day.
1975: campaigning publisher Juanita Nielsen vanishes after fighting Kings Cross developers — her body never found.
1980: the Nugan Hand merchant bank collapses amid drugs, arms-dealing and spies — a scandal that reached all the way to the CIA.
1984: a Father's Day swap-meet at Milperra erupts into a bikie gun battle that leaves seven dead — and rewrites the gun laws.

title: Modern Sydney: 1900 to Today
blurb: The twentieth-century city, in order — Federation, the Bridge, the war, the Opera House and the Games.

stops.note: 1 January 1901: in Centennial Park the colonies federate. The Commonwealth of Australia is born, and Sydney enters the new century as a state capital.
The boom doesn't last. In the 1931 Depression, families thrown out of their homes barricade the doors and fight the police across the inner suburbs.
1932: through the worst of it, the Harbour Bridge is finished — the 'Coathanger' that joins the city and lifts its spirits.
At its opening a sword-wielding zealot gallops up and slashes the ribbon before the Premier can — the strangest moment in the city's story.
1935: across the water beneath the bridge, Luna Park opens its grinning face. Just for fun.
31 May 1942: the war reaches home as three Japanese midget submarines slip into the harbour by night.
1956: 'Welcome to television.' From a studio at Willoughby, TCN-9 brings the flickering future into Sydney's living rooms.
1971: a fight to save a last patch of bush at Hunters Hill invents the 'green ban' — workers refusing to wreck the city.
1973: after sixteen years of genius and scandal, Utzon's sails open at Bennelong Point — and the world learns Sydney's face.
1988: for the Bicentenary the monorail loops the city centre — a future that never quite arrived, and was pulled down in 2013.
The same year the worn-out docks of Darling Harbour reopen as a waterfront of crowds and concrete.
2000: on a cleaned-up industrial wasteland at Homebush, Sydney stages the Olympic Games — its grand debut to the new millennium.

title: Women Who Made Sydney
blurb: From an Eora fisherwoman to a UN delegate — the women who shaped the city, in their turn.

stops.note: 1790s: the Cammeraygal fisherwoman Barangaroo refuses the colony entirely — its clothes, its food, its cruelties — the first woman on this map to say no to it.
1810s: transported at thirteen for horse theft, Mary Reibey builds a trading and property empire to become the colony's merchant queen — and the face of the $20 note.
1820s: a Darug woman raised in the Native Institution, Maria Lock fights through the colony's own courts to hold her family's land.
1840s: Caroline Chisholm meets the migrant ships and shelters thousands of single women, shaming the colony into caring for its newcomers.
From 1866, Mary MacKillop builds the Sisters of St Joseph and schools for the poor — excommunicated in her day, later made Australia's first saint.
1888: Louisa Lawson founds The Dawn, a journal written and printed by women, and helps lead the fight for the vote.
Through the 1890s, from her Woollahra salon, Rose Scott drives the suffrage campaign that wins NSW women the vote in 1902.
1908: a young Sydney woman, Dorothea Mackellar, gives the country its best-loved poem — 'I love a sunburnt country'.
1915: Lillian Armfield becomes one of Australia's first policewomen, and for decades takes on the razor gangs of the underworld.
1920s: from Mosman, the modernist Margaret Preston puts native wildflowers — and a call for a truly Australian art — at the centre of the canvas.
1925: Millicent Preston-Stanley becomes the first woman elected to the NSW Parliament, championing women's and children's health.
The underworld has its queens too: Kate Leigh runs the sly-grog and cocaine of Surry Hills...
...and her bitter rival Tilly Devine the brothels of Darlinghurst — two women ruling Sydney's vice through the razor years.
1945: Jessie Street, the only woman in Australia's delegation, helps write women's rights into the founding charter of the United Nations.

title: On the Move: Trains, Trams & Ferries
blurb: How Sydney got around — from the first steam train to the tunnels, ghost lines and the monorail that didn't last.

stops.note: 1855: the first train steams from Sydney to Parramatta — the colony enters the railway age.
1869: a Gothic 'mortuary station' beside Central sends Sydney's dead out to Rookwood by funeral train.
1902: the Pyrmont swing bridge opens across Darling Harbour — among the first electrically-operated swing spans in the world.
The Northern Beaches are promised trains for a century, and bridges built ready — but the line never comes.
1926: the City Circle is dug beneath the streets; unfinished tunnels at St James fill with water — an underground lake to this day.
1932: the Harbour Bridge carries road, rail and tram across the water in a single leap.
Beneath Wynyard, platforms built for the bridge's trams are abandoned when the trams are scrapped — ghost stations under the city.
On the water, the tradition that needs no track: the Manly ferry, 'seven miles from Sydney and a thousand miles from care'.
1958: the Cahill Expressway throws an elevated road across Circular Quay — loved and loathed ever since.
1979: after nearly a century of plans, the Eastern Suburbs Railway finally reaches Bondi Junction.
1988: the Bicentennial monorail loops Darling Harbour — a future that never caught on, torn down in 2013.
1992: a road tunnel dives beneath the harbour to take the load off the bridge.
1995: the cable-stayed Anzac Bridge soars over Johnstons Bay — the city's newest landmark crossing.

title: Sydney on Stage
blurb: A century of footlights — the playhouses, picture palaces and dance halls that lit up the city.

stops.note: 1886: 'the Cri' opens on Pitt Street, a thousand-seat playhouse for opera and melodrama.
1887: Her Majesty's brings grand musical theatre to town — and will twice burn down and rise again.
Through the 1890s, Sydney's own Nellie Stewart reigns as queen of the colonial stage — 'Sweet Nell'.
1893: the Tivoli becomes the home of vaudeville — variety, comedy and showgirls for half a century.
1924: the Prince Edward, 'the theatre beautiful', ushers in the picture-palace age.
1928: the Capitol opens in Haymarket as an atmospheric picture palace, stars twinkling in its ceiling.
The same year the Regent on George Street becomes the city's grandest cinema.
1929: the State Theatre out-dazzles them all — a Gothic-Baroque palace of marble and crystal.
1936: the Trocadero brings the dance-hall age, its sprung floor swinging right through the war.
1976: the Theatre Royal reopens inside the new MLC Centre, carrying the city's oldest theatrical name into the modern age.

title: Sydney's Grand Houses
blurb: The mansions of the colonial and Victorian rich — from Wentworth's Vaucluse to a Spanish-Mission fortune on Elizabeth Bay.

stops.note: 1827: W. C. Wentworth's battlemented Vaucluse House, harbourside seat of the colony's most powerful native-born family.
1835: out on the Cowpastures, the Macarthurs build Camden Park — grand house of the family that founded the wool industry.
1837: John Verge's elegant Tusculum, one of the villas that made fashionable Potts Point.
Verge's Lyndhurst at Glebe rises the same year — a Greek Revival gem later nearly lost to a freeway.
1839: Alexander Macleay's Elizabeth Bay House, 'the finest house in the colony', with its famous oval saloon and stair.
1840s: at Mulgoa, the sandstone grandeur of Fernhill, heart of a great pastoral estate.
1856: the Italianate Strickland House looks out over the harbour at Vaucluse.
1876: Hughenden, a Victorian mansion at Woollahra, later reborn as a grand hotel.
1882: the castellated Swifts at Darling Point — a brewer's fairy-tale castle on the harbour.
1885: Caerleon at Bellevue Hill, among the first of the great Queen Anne mansions in Australia.
1926: and the lavish Spanish-Mission 'Boomerang' on Elizabeth Bay, built on a music fortune — long one of Sydney's most valuable homes.
